title: Variance Reduction Techniques

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Standard MC has variance Var(estimator) = sigma^2 / M. We want to reduce sigma^2 (the per-path variance) without bias.
rules: Antithetic: pair (eps, -eps) per path; works when payoff is monotone in noise (Var(Y(eps) + Y(-eps))/2 < Var(Y)/2 if cov < 0).
Control variate: subtract a correlated estimator with known mean. X* = Xhat + (Y - Yhat).
Importance sampling: sample from g(x) instead of f(x), reweight by f(x)/g(x). Critical for rare events (deep-OTM).
Moment matching: rescale samples to enforce sample mean = population mean.
setup.question: Match each technique to its principle and when it applies.

learningObjectives: Identify when antithetic helps (monotonicity / negative correlation).
Apply control variates: estimate mean of correlated quantity, exploit known closed form.
Recognize importance sampling for tail events.
Use Quasi-Monte Carlo for low-dimensional smooth integrands.

stages.label: Antithetic
stages.prompt: When does the antithetic estimator (Y(eps) + Y(-eps))/2 have LOWER variance than averaging two independent draws?

options.text: When Cov(Y(eps), Y(-eps)) < 0. Then Var of the average is (Var(Y) + Cov)/2 < Var(Y)/2.

stages.explanation: Antithetic works when the payoff function is monotone in the noise (or negatively correlated under flipping). For a vanilla call payoff max(S_T - K, 0), Y is monotone increasing in eps, so Y(eps) and Y(-eps) are negatively correlated. Variance reduction can be 2x or more.

stages.label: Control variate
stages.prompt: Use a control variate Y with known mean E[Y] = mu_Y. The corrected estimator is X* = Xhat + beta * (mu_Y - Yhat). What's the optimal beta?

options.text: beta* = Cov(X, Y) / Var(Y).  Variance reduction factor: 1 - rho_XY^2.

stages.explanation: Optimal beta minimizes Var(X*). Solving: beta* = Cov(X, Y) / Var(Y). The remaining variance is Var(X) (1 - rho^2). High correlation between target and control variate means big variance reduction. Example: control an Asian option with the geometric Asian, which has closed form.

stages.label: Importance sampling
stages.prompt: Pricing a deep-OTM call where most paths have payoff 0. Why is importance sampling powerful here?

options.text: Sample from a tilted distribution g (e.g., shift the Gaussian mean toward the strike). Most paths now produce nonzero payoffs. Reweight by f/g to keep the estimator unbiased. Variance can be orders of magnitude smaller.
It increases the number of paths.
It doesn't help for OTM options.

stages.explanation: If only 1 in 1000 paths under f produces a nonzero payoff, you waste ~99.9% of the simulation. Tilt the distribution to put more mass where the payoff is positive, then reweight. Critical for credit risk, exotic structured products, and tail-event pricing.

stages.label: Commit
stages.template: Antithetic: payoff __[a]__ in noise.  Control variate: Y has __[b]__ mean and high __[c]__ with X.  Importance sampling: rare-event / __[d]__ payoff.  QMC: __[e]__-dimensional smooth integrals.
stages.explanation: Antithetic: monotone payoffs. Control variate: needs a correlated quantity with known mean. Importance sampling: rare events / OTM. QMC: low-dim smooth integrals (high-dim still suffers curse of dim).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why doesn't antithetic always help? (Non-monotone payoffs like straddles can have positive correlation between Y(eps) and Y(-eps).)
Why is QMC's O(1/M) convergence not always achievable? (It depends on smoothness and effective dimension; for high-dim non-smooth integrands, QMC degrades to MC's O(1/sqrt(M)).)
Combine techniques: stratified sampling + antithetic + control variate. Common in production pricing engines.
